perturbation.original_text: 1. The term “Confidential information” for the purpose of this Agreement shall mean the said Submissions and each concept, idea, game-play mechanic, set design, business model, and/or other element contained therein and any and all other proprietary and/or any other information and/or data which is provided and/or obtained hereunder weather in relation to the submission and/or otherwise, whether in writing, pictorially, in machine readable form, orally or by observation during their interactions/discussions, in connection with the Purpose or otherwise, including but not limited to, all intangible and tangible information, documents, data, papers, statements, any business/customer information and trade secrets relating to its business practices in connection with the Purpose or otherwise, and will form a part of the proprietary and confidential information weather disclosed by the Disclosing and/or Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. The term “Confidential information” for the purpose of this Agreement shall mean only the said Submissions in writing and no other information or data provided or obtained in any other format, including but not limited to orally or by observation during their interactions/discussions. Any other information, including trade secrets and business practices, shall not be considered Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This modification severely restricts the definition of "Confidential Information", potentially excluding trade secrets and other proprietary information that would normally be protected under trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_citation: Section 2(1)(r) of the Trade Secrets Act of India (Draft)
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text of the NDA includes broad proprietary information within the definition of confidential information. The modified text excludes trade secrets and business practices from being considered confidential, which directly contradicts the intention of the Trade Secret law to protect such information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: oncluded supply contracts with groceries producers which frequently contained clauses obliging the producers to provide a "full-scale service". Czech Republic Antitrust/Competition Law Vojtech Chloupek Your Author LinkedIn Connections To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. The Czech Office for the Protection of Competition (the " Office ") has concluded two proceedings with groceries store chain HRU`KA, spol. s.r.o. (" HRU`KA "), following an investigation into potential abuse of a dominant market position. The investigated practice entailed negotiating contract provisions for a so-called "full-scale service", forcing suppliers to buy back food products nearing their expiry date. After HRU`KA committed itself to compensate suppliers for all payments made under these provisions, the Office suspended the proceedings without imposing a fine on the store chain. From 2016 until 2021, HRU`KA concluded supply contracts with groceries producers which frequently contained clauses obliging the producers to provide a "full-scale service". The service was based on allowing HRU`KA to return purchased food products back to the suppliers at the end of their expiry date, or to extend a discount onto these unsold products through an accounting operation. The suppliers were contractually obliged to re-purchase their produce and to bear the costs of the transaction. According to the Office, this practice constituted a potential abuse of a dominant position, as HRU`KA was able to shift the risks and losses of selling groceries with expiry dates onto their suppliers entirely. The Office also found that HRU`KA proceeded to apply these provisions in practice with numerous suppliers. To avoid a potential fine, HRU`KA offered to remedy the situation by compensating 74 of its suppliers, who have been affected by the "full-scale service" provisions and made return payments to HRU`KA. The total to be returned amounts to over CZK 39 million (approx. EUR 1.5 million), to be paid within 8 months. Furthermore, HRU`KA has committed to remove all "full-scale service" clauses from its contracts and to provide the Office with a list of internal measures to be implemented. These must ensure that provisions in breach of abuse of dominant position regulation do not find their way into the company's future supply contracts. Under the condition that these commitments are fulfilled, the Office suspended the proceedings. The decision illustrates clear limits of risk sharing in supply-chain relations, as well as indicating the benefits of cooperation in investigation proceedings, potentially enabling alternative solutions to fine imposing. The content of this article is intended to provide a general guide to the subject matter. Specialist advice should be sought about your specific circumstances. perturbation.justification: The original contract text defines "Confidential Information" broadly, including various elements and data. The changed text severely restricts this definition to only written submissions, explicitly excluding trade secrets and business practices. The Law Explanation states that excluding trade secrets contradicts the intention of trade secret law. The provided snippets do not mention trade secrets, but the LLM explanation directly addresses the legal contradiction, which is enough to determine that this definition weakens legal protections and constitutes a contradiction, because trade secrets and business practices are typically considered confidential in business and legal contexts.

perturbation.original_text: 3. The Receiving shall use the Confidential information only for the Purpose and not disclose any of the Confidential Information to any third party without the Disclosing Party’s prior written consent, and in addition to the same the Receiving Party will only share such Confidential Information with its internal employees only and strictly on a need to know basis.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. The Receiving shall use the Confidential information only for the Purpose and may disclose the Confidential Information to any third party as they deem necessary, without the need for the Disclosing Party’s consent. The Receiving Party may also share such Confidential Information with any individuals or entities, regardless of whether they are internal employees or have a need to know.
perturbation.explanation: This clause now permits unrestricted disclosure of Confidential Information, nullifying the core purpose of a Non-Disclosure Agreement and potentially violating contractual and fiduciary duties.
perturbation.law_citation: Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act deals with compensation for loss or damage caused by breach of contract. The original NDA protects against unauthorized disclosure, but the modified text allows such disclosure, negating the confidentiality agreement and potentially causing damages. If the receiving party discloses and causes damages, the original clause would allow the disclosing party to claim damages, but the changed clause would not.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Compensation of loss or damage caused by breach of contract | Indian Contract Act, 1872 | Bare Acts | Law Library | AdvocateKhoj Follow @SCJudgments Login : Advocate | Client Indian Contract Act, 1872 73. Compensation of loss or damage caused by breach of contract When a contract has been broken, the party who suffers by such breach is entitled to receive, from the party who has broken the contract, compensation for any loss or damage caused to him thereby, which naturally arose in the usual course of things from such breach, or which the parties knew, when they made the contract, to be likely to result from the breach of it. Such compensation is not to be given for any remote and indirect loss or damage sustained by reason of the breach. Compensation for failure to discharge obligation resembling those created by contract: When an obligation resembling those created by contract has been incurred and has not been discharged, any person injured by the failure to discharge it is entitled to receive the same compensation from the party in default, as if such person had contracted to discharge it and had broken his contract. Explanation: In estimating the loss or damage arising from a breach of contract, the means which existed of remedying the inconvenience caused by non-performance of the contract must be taken into account. Illustrations (a) A contracts to sell and deliver 50 maunds of saltpetre to B, at a certain price to be paid on delivery. A breaks his promise. B is entitled to receive from A, by way of compensation, the sum, if any, by which the contract price falls short of the price for which B might have obtained 50 maunds of saltpetre of like quality at the time when the saltpetre ought to have been delivered. (b) A hires B's ship to go to Bombay, and there takes on board, on the first of January, a cargo, which A is to provide, and to bring it to Calcutta, the freight to be paid when earned. B's ship does not go to Bombay, but A has opportunities for procuring suitable conveyance for the cargo upon terms as advantageous as those on which he h
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original intent and purpose of the NDA, which is to protect confidential information. The original text restricts disclosure, while the changed text permits unrestricted disclosure to any third party. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, deals with compensation for loss or damage caused by breach of contract. By allowing disclosure, the changed text negates the protection offered by the original NDA, potentially causing damages without recourse. This creates a legal contradiction because the changed text removes the ability to claim damages for unauthorized disclosure, directly conflicting with the principles outlined in Section 73 regarding breach of contract and compensation for damages.

perturbation.original_text: 8. The Receiving party acknowledges that damages are not a sufficient remedy for the Disclosing Party for any breach of any of the Receiving Party’s undertakings herein provided and the Receiving Party further acknowledges that the Disclosing party is entitled to specific performance or injunctive relief (as appropriate) as a remedy for any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement and/or any undertakings hereunder by the Receiving Party, in addition to and without prejudice any other remedies available to the Disclosing Party in law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. The Receiving party acknowledges that monetary damages are the sole and sufficient remedy for the Disclosing Party for any breach of the Receiving Party’s obligations. The Disclosing party is not entitled to specific performance or injunctive relief under any circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting remedies to only monetary damages and removing specific performance and injunctive relief, the agreement weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to protect its Confidential Information effectively.
perturbation.law_citation: Section 41 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 41 of the Specific Relief Act outlines cases where an injunction cannot be granted. While the original text preserves the disclosing party's right to seek injunctive relief, the modified text explicitly denies it. This restricts the disclosing party's remedies, potentially allowing irreparable harm to occur without adequate recourse beyond monetary damages.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows the Disclosing Party to seek specific performance or injunctive relief, while the changed text explicitly denies this right, stating that monetary damages are the sole remedy. The Law Explanation indicates that Section 41 of the Specific Relief Act concerns when injunctions cannot be granted, and the modified text directly contradicts the possibility of injunctive relief. This direct contradiction is a substantial change from the original agreement, thus a legal contradiction exists because the contract language deviates from legally recognized enforcement options.

perturbation.original_text: 13. This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of India.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of [Country X], regardless of where the Confidential Information was disclosed or where the parties are located.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to that of a completely different country introduces uncertainty, particularly if that country's laws regarding confidentiality are weaker or non-existent compared to Indian law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Conflict of Laws Principles under Indian Law
perturbation.law_citation: Private International Law Principles recognized by Indian Courts
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can choose a governing law, Indian courts consider various factors to determine if the choice is bona fide and not intended to evade Indian law. A completely unrelated foreign law may be disregarded if it conflicts with public policy or if the contract has significant connections to India. Enforceability is very uncertain.
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the governing law from India to '[Country X]', creating a potential contradiction. The Law Explanation states that Indian courts consider whether the choice of governing law is bona fide and not intended to evade Indian law. Changing to a completely unrelated foreign law makes enforceability uncertain, particularly if the contract has significant connections to India, which is implied by the original governing law clause. Since no law snippets were helpful, I am relying on the Law and Perturbation Explanations.

perturbation.original_text: 14. The Parties hereto undertake that any dispute which may arise between them shall first be dealt with in the manner stated below, irrespective of the other recourse, which any Party may have in law or in equity. 14.1 If any dispute arises between the Parties hereto during the subsistence of this Agreement or thereafter, in connection with the validity, interpretation, implementation or alleged material breach of any provision of this Agreement or regarding a question, including the questions as to whether the termination of this Agreement by one Party hereto has been legitimate, both Parties hereto shall endeavour to settle such dispute amicably. If the Parties fail to bring about an amicable settlement within a period of thirty (30) days, either Party to the dispute may give ten (10) days notice of invocation of dispute settlement by the Film Writers Association, Mumbai, to the other Party in writing.The Parties hereto shall submit to such mediation award by the Film Writers Association and the award shall be enforceable in any competent court of law in Mumbai.
perturbation.changed_text: 14. Any dispute arising under this Agreement shall be resolved solely through a coin toss, the result of which will be final and binding on both parties. There shall be no recourse to any other form of dispute resolution, including but not limited to mediation or litigation.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the structured dispute resolution mechanism with a coin toss is not legally recognized as a valid or enforceable method of dispute resolution, and it directly contradicts established legal principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, and Civil Procedure Code, 1908
perturbation.law_citation: Section 7 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 7 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, outlines the requirements for a valid arbitration agreement. A coin toss is not a recognized method of arbitration or any other legally accepted form of dispute resolution, and it fails to meet the minimum requirements for a binding dispute resolution mechanism. This change undermines the enforceability of the agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specified a structured dispute resolution mechanism, involving mediation and potential enforcement in a court of law. The changed text replaces this with a coin toss, which is not a legally recognized or enforceable method of dispute resolution. The Perturbation Explanation notes that this directly contradicts established legal principles. The Law Explanation references Section 7 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, implying that a coin toss fails to meet the requirements for a valid arbitration agreement or any legally accepted form of dispute resolution. Although there are no scraped law snippets provided to directly confirm this, the overall effect is a clear departure from legally sound dispute resolution practices, making the contract unenforceable in that regard.
